subject: Chemistry
q: For the reaction system: $2 \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}_2(\mathrm{~g})$ volume is suddenly reduce to half its value by increasing the pressure on it. If the reaction is of first order with respect to $\mathrm{O}_2$ and second order with respect to $\mathrm{NO}$, the rate of reaction will
question: For the reaction system: $2 \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}_2(\mathrm{~g})$ volume is suddenly reduce to half its value by increasing the pressure on it. If the reaction is of first order with respect to $\mathrm{O}_2$ and second order with respect to $\mathrm{NO}$, the rate of reaction will
options: diminish to one-eighth of its initial value
increase to eight times of its initial value
increase to four times of its initial value
diminish to one-fourth of its initial value
solution:
$\mathrm{r}=\mathrm{k}\left[\mathrm{O}_2\right][\mathrm{NO}]^2$. When the volume is reduced to $1 / 2$, The conc. will double.
$\therefore$ New rate $=\mathrm{k}\left[2 \mathrm{O}_2\right][2 \mathrm{NO}]^2=8 \mathrm{k}\left[\mathrm{O}^2\right][\mathrm{NO}]^2$. The new rate increases by eight times.

If half life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>14</mn></mmultiscripts></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5730</mn></math> years, the age of wood sample is ..... years.

solution:
Given half life of C14= 5730 years.

λt=lnC14/C12atmosphere C14/C12wood sample 

As per the question,

C14/C12woodC14/C12atmosphere=18

So, λt=ln8

ln 2t1/2t=ln 8

t=3×t1/2=3×5730=17190 years

q:
The decomposition of formic acid on gold surface follows first order kinetics. If the rate constant at 300 K is 1.0×10-3 s-1 and the activation energy Ea=11.488 kJ mol-1, the rate constant at 200 K is _________ ×10-5 s-1. (Round of to the Nearest Integer).

(Given R=8.314 J mol-1 K-1)
question:
The decomposition of formic acid on gold surface follows first order kinetics. If the rate constant at 300 K is 1.0×10-3 s-1 and the activation energy Ea=11.488 kJ mol-1, the rate constant at 200 K is _________ ×10-5 s-1. (Round of to the Nearest Integer).

(Given R=8.314 J mol-1 K-1)
solution:
K300=10-3   K200=?

Ea=11.488KJ/mole  R=8.314 J/mole-K

so ℓnK300 K200=EaR1200-1300

ℓnK300 K200=11.488×1000×1008.314×200×300

=2.303

=ℓn10

so K300 K200=10

K200=110×K300=10-4

=10×10-5 sec-1

question: Catalyst A reduces the activation energy for a reaction by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>. The ratio of rate constants, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>Catalysed</mi></mrow><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>Uncatalysed</mi></mrow></mfrac></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is____[nearest integer] [Assume that the pre-exponential factor is same in both the cases. Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>31</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
solution:
Arrhenius equation for the rate constant of a reaction in terms of energy of activation is given by

K=Ae-EaRT

Kcatalyst=AeEa'RT

Kuncatalysed=Ae-EaRT

KcatalystKUncatalysed=eEa-Ea'RT=e10×10008.314×300=e4

question:

solution:
For first order reaction,

K=2.303tlogNoNt ... 1

Also, K=0.693t12 ... 2

Equating 1 and 2

Hence, 2.303tlogNoNt=0.693t12

2.30383logNoNt=0.693200

NoNt=43

%NtN0=0.75×100

=75%

subject: Chemistry
q: Higher order (>3) reactions are rare due to:
question: Higher order (>3) reactions are rare due to:
options: Loss of active species on collision.
Low probability of simultaneous collision of all the reacting species.
Increase in entropy and activation energy as more molecules are involved.
Shifting of equilibrium towards reactants due to elastic collisions.
solution: Probability of an event involving more than three molecules in a collision are remote.

The reaction of ozone with oxygen atoms in the presence of chlorine atoms can occur by a two step process shown below:

O3g+Cl•→O2g+ClO•g ...(i)

ki=5.2×109 L mol-1 s-1

ClO•g+O•g→O2g+Cl•g ...(ii)

kii=2.6×1010 L mol-1 s-1

The closest rate constant for the overall reaction

O3g+O•g→2O2g is:
question:
The reaction of ozone with oxygen atoms in the presence of chlorine atoms can occur by a two step process shown below:

O3g+Cl•→O2g+ClO•g ...(i)

ki=5.2×109 L mol-1 s-1

ClO•g+O•g→O2g+Cl•g ...(ii)

kii=2.6×1010 L mol-1 s-1

The closest rate constant for the overall reaction

O3g+O•g→2O2g is:
solution:
O3g+Clg•→O2g+ClOs•KiClOg•+Og•→O2g+Clg•Kii

_________________________________

O3g+Og•→202gKRate=Ki×Kii

Overall Rate constant   = Ki × Kii

= 5.2 × 109 × 2.6 × 1010

≈ 1.4 × 1020 mol-1 L s-1

subject: Chemistry
q: For the equilibrium, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mfenced><mo>⇌</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>∆</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mo> </mo><mi>is</mi><mo> </mo><mo>-</mo><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>mol</mi></math> . If the ratio of the activation energies of the forward <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi></msub></mfenced></math> and reverse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></msub></mfenced></math> reactions is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>2</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> then:
question: For the equilibrium, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mfenced><mo>⇌</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>∆</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mo> </mo><mi>is</mi><mo> </mo><mo>-</mo><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>mol</mi></math> . If the ratio of the activation energies of the forward <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi></msub></mfenced></math> and reverse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></msub></mfenced></math> reactions is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>2</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> then:
solution:
Activation energy is the minimum amount of energy that must be provided for compounds to result in a chemical reaction.

Ag⇌Bg ; ∆H=-40 kJ/mole

∆H=Eaf-Eab=-40 kJ

EafEab=23

⇒ Eaf=23Eab

⇒23 Eab-Eab=-Eab3=-40 kJ

Eab=+120 kJ

Eaf=23×120=80 kJ

subject: Chemistry
q:
NO2 required for a reaction is produced by decomposition of N2O5 in CCl4 as by equation 2N2O5( g)→4NO2( g)+O2( g)
The initial concentration of N2O5 is 3 mol L-1 and it is 2.75 mol L-1 after 30 minutes.
The rate of formation of NO2 is x×10-3 mol L-1 min-1, value of x is ________.
question:
NO2 required for a reaction is produced by decomposition of N2O5 in CCl4 as by equation 2N2O5( g)→4NO2( g)+O2( g)
The initial concentration of N2O5 is 3 mol L-1 and it is 2.75 mol L-1 after 30 minutes.
The rate of formation of NO2 is x×10-3 mol L-1 min-1, value of x is ________.
solution:
The given reaction is,

2 N2O5( g)→4NO2( g)+O2( g)

The initial concentration of N2O5 is 3 mol L-1 and it is 2.75 mol L-1 after 30 minutes.

Now, 

-ΔN2O5Δt=-(2.75-3)30-0 mol L-1 min-1=0.2530

12×ΔN2O5Δt=14 -ΔNO2Δt-ΔNO2Δt=0.2530×2=1.66×10-2 M/Min

≃17×10-3 M/Min

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the following single step reaction in gas phase at constant temperature.
\(2 \mathrm{~A}_{(\mathrm{g})}+\mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{g})}\)
The initial rate of the reaction is recorded as \(\mathrm{r}_1\) when the reaction starts with \(1.5 \mathrm{~atm}\) pressure of \(\mathrm{A}\) and \(0.7 \mathrm{~atm}\) pressure of \(\mathrm{B}\). After some time, the rate \(\mathrm{r}_2\) is recorded when the pressure of \(\mathrm{C}\) becomes \(0.5 \mathrm{~atm}\). The ratio \(\mathrm{r}_1: \mathrm{r}_2\) is ______ \(\times 10^{-1}\). (Nearest integer)
question:
Consider the following single step reaction in gas phase at constant temperature.
\(2 \mathrm{~A}_{(\mathrm{g})}+\mathrm{B}_{(\mathrm{g})} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{g})}\)
The initial rate of the reaction is recorded as \(\mathrm{r}_1\) when the reaction starts with \(1.5 \mathrm{~atm}\) pressure of \(\mathrm{A}\) and \(0.7 \mathrm{~atm}\) pressure of \(\mathrm{B}\). After some time, the rate \(\mathrm{r}_2\) is recorded when the pressure of \(\mathrm{C}\) becomes \(0.5 \mathrm{~atm}\). The ratio \(\mathrm{r}_1: \mathrm{r}_2\) is ______ \(\times 10^{-1}\). (Nearest integer)
solution:
$2 \mathrm{~A}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{B}(\mathrm{g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}(\mathrm{g})$
$\begin{array}{ll} \mathrm{r}_1 & 1.5 \mathrm{~atm} \quad 0.7 \mathrm{~atm} \\ \mathrm{r}_2 & 0.5 \mathrm{~atm} \quad 0.2 \mathrm{~atm} \quad 0.5 \mathrm{~atm} \\ & \because \mathrm{r}=\mathrm{K}\left[\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}\right]^2\left[\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}\right] \\ & \mathrm{r}_1=\mathrm{K}[1.5]^2[0.7] \\ & \mathrm{r}_2=\mathrm{K}[0.5]^2[0.2] \\ & \frac{\mathrm{r}_1}{\mathrm{r}_2}=9 \times \frac{7}{2}=31.5=315 \times 10^{-1} \end{array}$ Ans. 315

q: For a reaction, consider the plot of ln k versus 1/T given in the figure. If the rate constant of this reaction at 400 K is 10-5s-1 , then the rate constant at 500 K is:
question: For a reaction, consider the plot of ln k versus 1/T given in the figure. If the rate constant of this reaction at 400 K is 10-5s-1 , then the rate constant at 500 K is:
solution:
ln k=ln A-EaRT=ln A-46061T1-1T2∴EAR=4606

ln k10-5=4606×500-400500×400

ln k10-5=4606×12000=2.303

ln k10-5=ln 10

k=10-4s-1

In the above first order reaction the concentration of PCl5 reduces from initial concentration 50 mol L-1 to 10 mol L-1 in 120 minutes at 300 K. The rate constant for the reaction at 300 K is x×10-2 min-1. The value of x is __________.

PCl5gI order 300 KPCl3g+Cl2g

t=0, [PCl5]=  50Mt=120 min, [PCl5]=  10M

t=120 min10M

⇒K=2.303tlogA0At

⇒K=2.303120log5010

q: If the activation energy of a reaction is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>80</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the fraction of molecules at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>700</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>, having enough energy to react to form products is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is (Rounded off to the nearest integer) [Use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>31</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
question: If the activation energy of a reaction is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>80</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the fraction of molecules at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>700</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>, having enough energy to react to form products is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is (Rounded off to the nearest integer) [Use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>31</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
solution:
Fraction of molecules to have enough energy to react =e-Ea/RT

So, x=EaRT

=80.9×1038.31×700

=13.9≈14

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider an elementary reaction
$\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{B}(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{C}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{D}(\mathrm{g})$
If the volume of reaction mixture is suddenly reduced to $\frac{1}{3}$ of its initial volume, the reaction rate will become ' $x$ ' times of the original reaction rate. The value of $x$ is :
question:
Consider an elementary reaction
$\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{B}(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{C}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{D}(\mathrm{g})$
If the volume of reaction mixture is suddenly reduced to $\frac{1}{3}$ of its initial volume, the reaction rate will become ' $x$ ' times of the original reaction rate. The value of $x$ is :
solution:
Since, the reaction is elementary
$\text { Rate }=k[A]^1[B]^1$
When V is reduced to $\frac{1}{3} \mathrm{~V}$, concentration will be tripled.
Hence, rate $=9 \times(\text { rate })_{\text {initial }}$
$x=9$

question:

=32(3.14)2

=315.5072

subject: Chemistry
q: If 50 % of a reaction occurs in 100 second and 75 % of the reaction occurs in 200 second, the order of this reaction is:
question: If 50 % of a reaction occurs in 100 second and 75 % of the reaction occurs in 200 second, the order of this reaction is:
solution:
50% of a reaction occurs in 100 second and 75% of the reaction occurs in 200 second, the order of this reaction is 1.

Let the initial reactant concentration is 1 M.

In the first 100 seconds, reactant concentration is reduced to 50% or 1×50100​=0.5 M.

In the second 100 seconds (total 200 seconds from start), reactant concentration is reduced to 100−75=25% or  1× 25100​ =0.25 M..

Thus, we observe that after each 100 second period, the reactant concentration is reduced to one half. Hence, 100 seconds is the half-life period and is independent of reactant concentration. This is characteristic of the first-order reaction.



First-order reaction as the half-life is constant.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the following reactions
A→P1;B→P2;C→P3;D→P4
The order of the above reactions are a, b, c and d,respectively. The following graph is obtained when log[rate] vs.log[conc.] are plotted:

  
Among the following, the correct sequence for the order of the reactions is :
question:
Consider the following reactions
A→P1;B→P2;C→P3;D→P4
The order of the above reactions are a, b, c and d,respectively. The following graph is obtained when log[rate] vs.log[conc.] are plotted:

  
Among the following, the correct sequence for the order of the reactions is :
solution:
Rate =K[Conc]order 

logRate =logK+ order× log[conc]
so slope of graph is order of reaction. greater the slope greater is order of reaction so order of reaction ⇒"c<a<b<d''

subject: Chemistry
q:
A person's wound was exposed to some bacteria and then bacteria growth started to happen at the same place. The wound was later treated with some antibacterial medicine and the rate of bacterial decay (r) was found to be proportional with the square of the existing number of bacteria at any instance. Which of the following set of graphs correctly represents the 'before' and 'after' situation of the application of the medicine?
[Given : $\mathrm{N}=$ No. of bacteria, $\mathrm{t}=$ time, bacterial growth follows $I^{\text {st }}$ order kinetics.]
question:
A person's wound was exposed to some bacteria and then bacteria growth started to happen at the same place. The wound was later treated with some antibacterial medicine and the rate of bacterial decay (r) was found to be proportional with the square of the existing number of bacteria at any instance. Which of the following set of graphs correctly represents the 'before' and 'after' situation of the application of the medicine?
[Given : $\mathrm{N}=$ No. of bacteria, $\mathrm{t}=$ time, bacterial growth follows $I^{\text {st }}$ order kinetics.]
solution:
*Before applying medicine
$\frac{\mathrm{dA}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{K}[\mathrm{A}]$ (First order growth) (Rate law)
$\frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{~A}_0}=\frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{~N}_0}=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{Kt}}$
*After applying medicine

$\mathrm{r}=-\frac{\mathrm{dA}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{K}[\mathrm{A}]^2 \quad$ (Rate law)
$y=\mathrm{Kx}^2$ Parabola

subject: Chemistry
q: Consider the following plots of log of rate constant $\mathrm{k}(\log \mathrm{k})$ vs $\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}}$ for three different reactions. The correct order of activation energies of these reactions is
question: Consider the following plots of log of rate constant $\mathrm{k}(\log \mathrm{k})$ vs $\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}}$ for three different reactions. The correct order of activation energies of these reactions is
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{K}=\mathrm{A} \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{E} a \mathrm{RT}} \\
& \operatorname{logk}=\log \mathrm{A}-\frac{\mathrm{Ea}}{2.303 \mathrm{RT}}
\end{aligned}$
For graph between logk with $\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}}$
$\mid \text { Slope of curve } \left\lvert\,=\frac{\mathrm{Ea}}{2.303 \mathrm{R}}\right.$
From given graph
Magnitude of slope $\Rightarrow(2) \gt (1) \gt (3)$
Hence $\mathrm{Ea}_2 \gt \mathrm{Ea}_1 \gt \mathrm{Ea}_3$

subject: Chemistry
q:
The following results were obtained during kinetic studies of the reaction.

2A+B→ product
 
Experiment A in mol L-1 B  in mol L-1 Initial rate of reaction in mol L-1 min -1
I 0.10 0.20 6.93×10-3
II 0.10 0.25 6.93×10-3
III 0.20 0.30 1.386×10-2

The time (in minutes) required to consume half of A is
question:
The following results were obtained during kinetic studies of the reaction.

2A+B→ product
 
Experiment A in mol L-1 B  in mol L-1 Initial rate of reaction in mol L-1 min -1
I 0.10 0.20 6.93×10-3
II 0.10 0.25 6.93×10-3
III 0.20 0.30 1.386×10-2

The time (in minutes) required to consume half of A is
solution:
Let's assume x and y are the order of the reaction with respect to A and B, respectively.

From equation 1 and 2,

0.10.1x0.20.25y=6.93×10-36.93×10-3⇒y=0

From equation 1 and 3 and put the value y=0,

0.10.2x0.20.30=6.93×10-31.386×10-2    ∴y=0

12x=12⇒x=1

The rate law will be R=K[A]1[B]0

Hence, the reaction is of the first order.

K=RA=6.93×10-30.1=6.93×10-2

Here t12 for the first-order reaction is given by, t12=0.693K=0.693(6.93×10-2)=10

subject: Chemistry
q:
The rate law for the reaction below is given by the expression kA[B]

A+B→Product

If the concentration of B is increased from 0.1 to 0.3 mol, keeping the value of A at 0.1 mol, the rate constant will be:
question:
The rate law for the reaction below is given by the expression kA[B]

A+B→Product

If the concentration of B is increased from 0.1 to 0.3 mol, keeping the value of A at 0.1 mol, the rate constant will be:
solution: Rate constant is independent of concentration. It depends only on temperature. But the rate of the reaction increases by three times as the order of the reaction is one.

q: For a first order reaction, $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{P}, \mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}$ (half-life) is 10 days. The time required for $\frac{1}{4}^{\text {th }}$ conversion of $\mathrm{A}$ (in days) is: $(\ln 2=0.693, \ln 3=1.1)$.
question: For a first order reaction, $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{P}, \mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}$ (half-life) is 10 days. The time required for $\frac{1}{4}^{\text {th }}$ conversion of $\mathrm{A}$ (in days) is: $(\ln 2=0.693, \ln 3=1.1)$.
